one_line_takeaway: SkillProbe is a working multi-agent auditing pipeline (Gatekeeper -> Alignment Detector -> Flow Simulator) that, when run against 2,500 real ClawHub skills, found over 90% failed a clean rigorous pass and that popularity does not predict security, but the paper offers no ground-truth-labeled evaluation of the auditor's own accuracy.
key_finding: Across the top 2,500 most-downloaded ClawHub skills, only 247 (9.9%) were fully clean (Approved, no findings); 59.8% passed but had minor findings; 30.4% (759 skills) were marked Conditional for security-rule violations -- i.e. over 90% of popular skills failed to reach a clean/rigorous pass. Critically, this Conditional rate stayed statistically invariant (roughly 20-35%) across all 25 download-rank bins, refuting the assumption that higher popularity correlates with better security ('popularity-security paradox'). Among the 499 skills with at least one confirmed risk-link exploit, the constructed risk network contained 75,373 risk edges and was fully connected as a single giant component; Data Exfiltration (32,328 edges) and Fact Poisoning (28,441 edges) accounted for about 80% of all edges, with Command Injection third (8,637 edges). In the cross-model study (Table 2), Claude Sonnet 4.6 was the strictest auditor (45% Conditional rate, 9/20 skills flagged Conditional, 228.6s avg time), Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite was fastest (18.5s, 90% Approved), and GPT-5.2-Codex was most permissive on verdicts (95% Approved) while issuing the most average recommendations (2.3) -- illustrating a 'stringency vs. granularity vs. latency' trilemma across models auditing the identical skill set. One skill (nano-banana-pro) was consistently flagged Conditional by all 8 models, evidencing reproducible systemic semantic deficiencies.
core_contribution: SkillProbe, a multi-stage, multi-agent security auditing framework for LLM 'agent skill' marketplaces (e.g., ClawHub). It adopts a 'Skills-for-Skills' paradigm: each auditing phase is itself packaged as a standardized skill module (natural-language spec, workflow config, system prompts, scripts) driving specialized LLM agents through a three-phase pipeline: (P1) Gatekeeper (admission filtering for malicious code patterns, hazardous dependencies/CVEs, permission-declaration sanity, combining deterministic checks and LLM analysis, with any single 'BLOCK' vetoing the whole skill); (P2) Alignment Detector (formalizes a skill's declared capabilities D(s) vs. implemented capabilities C(s), each a <Resource, Access> tuple, and classifies each skill into a four-class matrix: Match, Over-declaration, Under-declaration/'shadow functions', Mixed); and (P3) Flow Simulator (tags skills with output-risk/input-sensitivity labels and matches them against 9 predefined risk-link policies covering source→sink attack patterns such as command injection, indirect prompt injection, data exfiltration, fact poisoning and intent hijacking, reducing combinatorial skill-pair search from O(2^N) brute force to O(N x |Rules|) rule matching). Final verdicts (REJECTED / CONDITIONAL / APPROVED) follow a one-vote-veto policy across three scorecard dimensions (malicious patterns, semantic consistency, composition safety). The authors built and deployed a working system (Holos-SkillHub: FastAPI backend, Vue 3 frontend, CLI, OpenClaw plugin) and used it to audit real ClawHub skills at scale.
threat_model: Attackers are third-party skill developers on open marketplaces (ClawHub) who publish skills that are semantically compliant (benign-looking documentation/description) but logically harmful, exploiting two specific gaps: (1) semantic-behavioral inconsistency, where a skill's SKILL.md description does not match its executable code (shadow functions, over-privilege) so that an agent's natural-language-anchored reasoning cannot perceive the deviation; and (2) inter-skill combinatorial risk, where two or more individually-benign skills are chained/composed (e.g., an upstream skill's output feeds a downstream skill's sensitive sink) to produce emergent attacks such as command injection, indirect prompt injection, data exfiltration, fact poisoning or intent hijacking that no single-skill audit would catch. This is explicitly framed as the inverse of prior doc-consistency work (which assumes documentation is authored in good faith); here the documentation itself is the attack vector.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 5): (1) Detection depth is bounded by the reasoning capability of the underlying LLM for highly obfuscated code or opaque third-party API responses (Code Extractor precision degrades). (2) Zero-day blind spot: the Flow Simulator's combinatorial risk detection depends on a predefined, fixed set of 9 risk-link policies (Table 1), so novel/undefined attack patterns outside those source-sink combinations are not detected. (3) Computational overhead / latency-stringency tradeoff remains unresolved at very high skill-submission volumes (>10,000). Additional gaps evident from the paper itself (not stated by authors as limitations but apparent from the evaluation design): there is no ground-truth-labeled malicious/benign dataset used to compute precision/recall/false-positive rate for SkillProbe's verdicts -- the large-scale audit numbers (9.9%/59.8%/30.4%) describe the framework's own verdict distribution on real skills with no independent oracle to validate correctness; there is no comparison against the two prior static-analysis baselines discussed in Related Work (Liu et al. 2026a/2026b) or against the ClawHub VirusTotal+LLM scanning pipeline it claims to improve upon; and the risk-link network claims (giant connected component, 75,373 edges) describe association between skills sharing a confirmed risk-chain type, which is a property of the audited-skill graph, not an externally validated exploit-chaining demonstration.

datasets_used: ClawHub marketplace skills as of March 16, 2026
Top 20 most-downloaded ClawHub skills (cross-model study, 160 total audits across 8 models)
Top 2,500 most-downloaded ClawHub skills (large-scale empirical audit)
Subset of 499 skills with at least one confirmed risk-link exploit (used to build the 75,373-edge risk network)
quantitative_results: Large-scale audit of top 2,500 skills: 247 skills (9.9%) fully clean/Approved w/o findings; 59.8% Approved w/ minor findings; 30.4% (759 skills) Conditional
Conditional rate stable at ~20-35% across all 25 download-rank bins (popularity not predictive of security)
Risk-link network over 499 confirmed-risk skills: 75,373 total risk edges, fully connected as a single giant component
Data Exfiltration edges: 32,328; Fact Poisoning edges: 28,441 (together ~80% of all edges); Command Injection edges: 8,637 (third-ranked)
Cross-model study (top 20 skills, Table 2): Claude Haiku 4.5 -- 86.7s avg time, 80% Approved/20% Conditional, 4 Warning/41 Info findings, 0.5 avg recs
Claude Sonnet 4.6 -- 228.6s avg time, 55% Approved/45% Conditional (highest Conditional rate, 9/20 skills), 10 Warning/82 Info findings, 1.7 avg recs
Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite -- 18.5s avg time (fastest), 90% Approved/10% Conditional, 2 Warning/34 Info findings, 0.6 avg recs
Gemini 3.1 Pro -- 221.8s avg time, 80% Approved/20% Conditional, 8 Warning/44 Info findings, 1.4 avg recs
GPT-5 mini -- 171.3s avg time, 85% Approved/15% Conditional, 9 Warning/88 Info findings, 1.9 avg recs
GPT-5.2-Codex -- 120.3s avg time, 95% Approved/5% Conditional (most permissive verdicts), 2 Warning/77 Info findings, 2.3 avg recs (highest)
Nex-N1 -- 38.7s avg time, 90% Approved/10% Conditional, 2 Warning/33 Info findings, 0.7 avg recs
Nex-N1.1 -- 282.0s avg time (slowest), 95% Approved/5% Conditional, 1 Warning/35 Info findings, 0.9 avg recs

Risk-link policy taxonomy: 9 attack patterns (Table 1) mapping output-risk sources (unsanitized external string, executable code fragments, sensitive credentials/data, manipulative semantics) to input-sensitivity sinks (shell/interpreter execution, LLM prompt injection, sensitive action parameter, external network egress, persistent state/config, sensitive action trigger)
baselines_compared: None directly benchmarked against SkillProbe within this paper -- Gatekeeper phase reuses the existing 'Skill Vetter' tool as its admission-filtering policy baseline, and Related Work contrasts SkillProbe conceptually (not empirically) with ClawHub's existing VirusTotal+LLM post-publication scanning pipeline and with two prior ecosystem-scale static-analysis studies (Liu et al. 2026a auditing 98,380 skills for two attacker archetypes; Liu et al. 2026b auditing 31,132 skills, finding 26.1% vulnerable across 14 patterns) -- these are cited comparators from Related Work, not baselines run against SkillProbe's own dataset in this paper's experiments
